content.text: Steward review cadence is the structured schedule and set of processes by which domain stewards regularly revisit, re-certify, or retire documents in an agentic AI knowledge base. It combines time-based review cycles (e.g., quarterly for policies, monthly for operational data) with event-triggered reviews (a policy change, an incident report, an audit finding) and defines the exception-handling procedures for situations that fall outside the standard schedule — such as urgent removals, steward unavailability, or bulk re-certification after a policy reissue. Together, the cadence and exception-handling rules ensure that the knowledge base degrades gracefully rather than silently.

Agentic AI systems are particularly sensitive to stale grounding data because agents chain multiple retrieval steps. A single outdated document retrieved early in an agent's reasoning can propagate incorrect assumptions across all subsequent steps. Unlike a search engine where a user sees multiple results and exercises judgment, an agent synthesises retrieved content into a single coherent answer — the user typically has no visibility into which source documents drove the response. This means the knowledge base must be proactively managed; users cannot self-correct for stale content.

Without a defined cadence, stewards default to reviewing documents only when prompted by an incident. The lag between when a document becomes stale and when an incident surfaces can be weeks or months, during which the AI system produces subtly incorrect outputs at scale. A formal cadence shifts the governance posture from reactive to proactive.
— stakeholders can state confidently that all knowledge base documents have been reviewed within the last N days, enabling service-level commitments about AI output quality.
— proactive cadence catches stale or superseded documents before they cause user-facing errors, reducing the volume and severity of AI response incidents.
— scheduled review logs provide regulators and internal auditors with a timestamped record of oversight activity, supporting compliance with AI governance frameworks.
— a defined cadence lets stewards plan review time in advance rather than facing unpredictable spikes caused by accumulated backlogs.
— documented exception procedures mean that urgent removals, deputy escalations, and bulk re-certifications follow a known path rather than ad-hoc workarounds.
— if a policy changes on day 15 of a 90-day cycle, the knowledge base carries the wrong version for up to 75 days unless an event-triggered review is also in place.
— when a large domain's annual policy reissue coincides with a scheduled cadence review, the steward faces a sudden large queue. Staggered certification dates mitigate this but require planning.
— too many ad-hoc extensions erode the integrity of the review cadence. If extensions become routine, the cadence is de facto longer than formally specified.
— documents owned by multiple stewards require coordinated re-certification. Misaligned schedules between co-owners can block a document from returning to the index after review.

The cadence operates on three tracks simultaneously. The scheduled track runs time-based review cycles: each document in the knowledge base has a 'next_review_at' date set at certification time based on the document category. A nightly job scans all certified documents and sends reminder notifications to stewards at 14 days before expiry, 7 days before expiry, and again on the expiry day. Documents that reach expiry without re-certification are automatically suspended from retrieval.

The event-triggered track monitors source systems for changes. When a source document is updated in the authoritative repository (SharePoint, Confluence, a document management system), a webhook event is fired to the KB governance service, which marks the corresponding certified chunk as pending re-review and notifies the domain steward. This prevents the 75-day lag described above.

The exception track handles three classes of exception: urgent removals (a steward or AI safety team member removes a document immediately, bypassing the scheduled cycle); review extensions (a steward requests a 14-day extension when they are temporarily unavailable, which an approver must grant and which is logged with a reason); and bulk re-certifications (when a top-level policy document is reissued, all downstream documents that cite it are flagged for simultaneous review).

attrs.alt: Steward review cadence timeline showing certification, scheduled reviews at day 30 and 60, 14-day warning at day 76, expiry at day 90, suspension at day 91, and exception handling paths feeding into an audit log.

content.text: The timeline shows the lifecycle of a single certified document. Event-triggered reviews (not shown on the timeline) can fire at any point between Day 0 and Day 90 when the source document changes. All three exception paths — urgent removal, extension request, bulk re-certification — funnel through the same audit log, ensuring a uniform evidence trail regardless of which path was taken.
Any production agentic AI system grounded in internal documents where the cost of stale information is significant — financial decisions, HR advice, legal guidance, operational procedures.
When the organisation has regulatory obligations to demonstrate ongoing oversight of AI training or grounding data.
When the knowledge base is large enough that ad-hoc steward attention cannot cover all documents, requiring a systematic scheduling mechanism.
When a previous incident revealed that a stale document caused an AI response error and the post-mortem identified cadence as the missing control.

— assign each document type a review period based on its rate of change. Example: regulatory documents = 30 days, policies = 90 days, architectural decisions = 180 days. Document these in a data dictionary or governance charter.
Store 'next_review_at' in the vector index metadata
— computed as 'certified_at + review_period_days'. This field is checked at retrieval time and used by the nightly staleness job.

— a scheduled job queries the index for documents where 'next_review_at' is within 14 days of now and sends Slack or email notifications to the relevant steward. On the expiry date, the job sets 'steward_approved = false' and fires an incident alert.
Implement webhook listeners for source system changes

— register webhooks on Confluence, SharePoint, or your document management system to fire when a source document is modified. The listener sets a 'pending_review' flag on the corresponding chunk and notifies the steward.

— publish written procedures for urgent removal (who can trigger it, within what SLA it must execute), extension requests (who approves, maximum extension duration, reason required), and bulk re-certification (what triggers it, who leads the batch review).

— build a dashboard showing mean days since last review per domain, fraction of documents within N days of expiry, extension rate by domain, and steward mean time to close a review. Surface these to governance leadership monthly.

# cadence/staleness_checker.py
# Nightly job: find documents near expiry and update certification status

from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Protocol
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

WARNING_DAYS = 14  # Send alert this many days before expiry


class DocumentStore(Protocol):
    """Minimal interface for the vector store metadata layer."""
    def find_expiring(self, before: datetime) -> list[dict]: ...
    def set_approved(self, doc_id: str, approved: bool) -> None: ...


class Notifier(Protocol):
    def send_warning(self, steward_email: str, doc: dict, days_left: int) -> None: ...
    def send_expiry_alert(self, steward_email: str, doc: dict) -> None: ...


def run_staleness_check(
    store: DocumentStore,
    notifier: Notifier,
    now: datetime | None = None,
) -> dict[str, int]:
    if now is None:
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    warning_threshold = now + timedelta(days=WARNING_DAYS)
    expiring_docs = store.find_expiring(before=warning_threshold)

    counts = {"warnings_sent": 0, "expired": 0, "ok": 0}

    for doc in expiring_docs:
        expiry = datetime.fromisoformat(doc["expires_at"])
        days_left = (expiry - now).days

        if days_left <= 0:
            # Document has expired: suspend from retrieval
            store.set_approved(doc["doc_id"], approved=False)
            notifier.send_expiry_alert(doc["steward_email"], doc)
            counts["expired"] += 1
            logger.warning("Suspended expired doc %s (steward=%s)",
                           doc["doc_id"], doc["steward_email"])
        elif days_left <= WARNING_DAYS:
            notifier.send_warning(doc["steward_email"], doc, days_left)
            counts["warnings_sent"] += 1
        else:
            counts["ok"] += 1

    return counts

# cadence/exception_handler.py
# Exception paths: urgent remove, extension request, bulk re-certification

from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
import uuid

class ExceptionType(Enum):
    URGENT_REMOVE = "urgent_remove"
    EXTENSION = "extension"
    BULK_RECERT = "bulk_recert"

@dataclass
class ExceptionRecord:
    exception_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    doc_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    exception_type: ExceptionType = ExceptionType.URGENT_REMOVE
    requested_by: str = ""
    approved_by: str | None = None
    reason: str = ""
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc))
    resolved_at: datetime | None = None


def urgent_remove(doc_ids: list[str], requested_by: str, reason: str,
                  store, audit_log) -> ExceptionRecord:
    """Immediately suspend documents from retrieval. No approval required."""
    for doc_id in doc_ids:
        store.set_approved(doc_id, approved=False)

    record = ExceptionRecord(
        doc_ids=doc_ids,
        exception_type=ExceptionType.URGENT_REMOVE,
        requested_by=requested_by,
        reason=reason,
        resolved_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
    )
    audit_log.write(record)
    return record


def request_extension(doc_id: str, steward_id: str, approver_id: str,
                      reason: str, extra_days: int,
                      store, audit_log) -> ExceptionRecord:
    """Grant a short extension to a document nearing expiry."""
    assert extra_days <= 14, "Maximum extension is 14 days"
    store.extend_expiry(doc_id, extra_days)
    record = ExceptionRecord(
        doc_ids=[doc_id],
        exception_type=ExceptionType.EXTENSION,
        requested_by=steward_id,
        approved_by=approver_id,
        reason=reason,
    )
    audit_log.write(record)
    return record

A mid-size financial services firm deployed an agentic AI assistant for its operations team. The assistant answered questions about compliance procedures, product eligibility rules, and settlement workflows. Initially, the team set a single 90-day review cadence for all documents. Within six months, two incidents occurred: the assistant cited a product eligibility rule that had been updated 45 days earlier (within the 90-day window), and a settlement procedure that had been superseded by a regulatory change 22 days after its certification.

The post-mortem surfaced two root causes: the cadence was too long for high-volatility document types, and there was no event-triggered review tied to source system changes. The firm redesigned the cadence with three tiers: regulatory documents (30-day review), operational procedures (60-day review), and reference documents (90-day review). They also connected the cadence system to their internal policy management tool via webhook, so any approved policy change automatically queued the corresponding KB document for re-review within 48 hours.

Over the following quarter, the time-to-fix for outdated grounding documents dropped from an average of 23 days (the lag between a change and the next scheduled review) to 2.1 days (the median time for a steward to process a webhook-triggered review alert). No further retrieval-driven incidents occurred in the following six months.
Single cadence for all document types
— applying a 90-day review to regulatory documents that change monthly creates systematic lag. Cadence must match document volatility.
— relying solely on scheduled cadence means source-system changes are not reflected until the next cycle, which can be weeks away.
Treating expiry as a hard cliff
— if the steward is on annual leave, an expiring document gets suspended with no warning to users. Use 14-day and 7-day warnings with deputy escalation to prevent surprise suspensions.
Extensions without time limits or reasons
— an extension process without a maximum duration and mandatory reason field becomes a way for stewards to perpetually defer reviews, undermining the cadence.
— if nobody tracks the fraction of documents within 14 days of expiry or the average days since last review, cadence degradation goes unnoticed until an incident surfaces it.
Stagger certification dates within a domain
— if all 200 HR documents were certified on the same day, their reviews all fall due simultaneously. Spread certification dates across the calendar to distribute steward workload evenly.
Use webhook-driven event triggers alongside scheduled cadence
— connect the cadence system to authoritative source systems so that document changes automatically queue a re-review, rather than waiting for the next scheduled cycle.
Cap extension duration and require approval
— maximum 14-day extensions, requiring a named approver. Log the reason. If a domain consistently needs extensions, it needs more steward capacity.
— when a top-level policy document changes, automatically flag all downstream KB documents that cite it for review. Do not rely on stewards to manually identify the impact chain.
Publish cadence metrics to governance leadership monthly
— mean days since last review, fraction of documents near expiry, and extension rate are leading indicators of governance health. Surface them before they become lagging incident metrics.

Cadence systems are IO-bound rather than compute-bound. The nightly staleness checker queries the metadata layer of the vector index — a fast metadata-only query without vector computation — so it scales to millions of documents without significant overhead. The bottleneck is human: the number of review tasks a steward can complete per day.

For large domains, batch review UIs that present groups of similar documents together (all documents of type 'expense policy', all documents from a given quarter) allow stewards to process 10–20 documents in a single review session rather than reviewing each individually. AI-assisted pre-screening — using an LLM to summarise what changed between the previous and current version of a source document — further reduces cognitive load, enabling stewards to review diffs rather than re-reading full documents.
On-call schedules map to deputy stewards
Query vector store metadata for metrics

The cadence system itself is a governance control that must be protected against both accidental and deliberate bypass. Key reliability requirements include: the nightly checker must be idempotent so that a failed run can be safely retried without double-suspending or double-notifying; extension grants must be logged with the approver's identity so that a pattern of unexplained extensions is detectable in audit; and the audit log of cadence events must be append-only and separate from operational tables that the checker writes to, preventing accidental truncation of the governance record.
— use the document's 'last_notified_at' timestamp to avoid sending duplicate warnings on re-runs.
Separate audit log from operational store
— write cadence events (extensions, removals, bulk re-certs) to an append-only audit table that the checker process cannot delete from.
— if the nightly checker fails to run, documents may expire without suspension. Monitor checker run health and alert on missed runs.
A formal review cadence shifts knowledge base governance from reactive (incident-driven) to proactive, bounding the maximum age of grounding content in the AI system.
Cadence periods must be calibrated to the rate of change of each document category — a single period for all document types is almost always wrong.
Event-triggered reviews (source system webhooks) are essential complements to scheduled cadence; they close the lag window when a source document changes between scheduled review dates.
Exception procedures — urgent removal, extension requests, bulk re-certification — must be documented, approved, and logged; undocumented exceptions accumulate into governance debt.
The nightly staleness checker is the core automation: it queries metadata, sends warnings, and suspends expired documents; it must be idempotent and monitored for reliability.

Cadence health metrics — mean days since last review, fraction near expiry, extension rate — are leading indicators; surface them to governance leadership before they manifest as AI response incidents.
